breaking_news.title: LeakBase Cybercrime Forum Seized in Global Takedown
breaking_news.summary:
Law enforcement agencies from 14 countries, led by the FBI and Europol, dismantled LeakBase, one of the world’s largest cybercrime forums used to buy and sell stolen credentials, stealer logs, and hacking tools. The operation seized two domains, arrested two individuals, and disrupted a central node in the cybercrime ecosystem that had over 142,000 registered users and more than 215,000 messages as of December 2025.

This takedown represents a major blow to the underground economy, as LeakBase functioned as a critical marketplace for credential stuffing, identity theft, and initial access brokering. Its removal limits threat actors’ ability to rapidly scale attacks using pre-compromised credentials, and signals increased international coordination in targeting cybercrime infrastructure.

breaking_news.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Flaws Actively Exploited in Wild
breaking_news.summary:
Cisco confirmed that vulnerabilities CVE-2026-20122 and CVE-2026-20128 in its Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (vManage) are under active exploitation in the wild. CVE-2026-20122 is an arbitrary file overwrite flaw (CVSS 7.1) that could allow authenticated remote attackers to compromise device integrity and deploy persistent backdoors. Cisco has issued emergency patches and urged immediate upgrades.

Given the widespread deployment of Cisco SD-WAN in enterprise and critical infrastructure networks globally, active exploitation poses a high risk of supply chain compromise, lateral movement, and disruption of network management. The vulnerabilities enable attackers to bypass authentication and gain deep network access, making this a top-tier operational risk.

breaking_news.title: Google: 90 Zero-Days Exploited in 2025, Record Enterprise Targeting
breaking_news.summary:
Google’s Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) tracked 90 zero-day vulnerabilities actively exploited in 2025—up from 78 in 2024—with 43 (48%) targeting enterprise software and appliances, a new high. The report highlights a structural shift toward enterprise exploitation, driven by commercial spyware vendors and state-sponsored actors, particularly those linked to China. Zero-day abuse in enterprise tech reached an all-time high, underscoring the growing sophistication and targeting of critical infrastructure and business-critical systems.

This trend signals that attackers are prioritizing persistent, high-impact access over broad, opportunistic campaigns. The rise in zero-day use by both nation-states and commercial vendors increases the risk of undetected intrusions and long-term compromise in Fortune 500 and government networks.

breaking_news.title: Coruna iOS Exploit Kit Proliferates Across Threat Actors
breaking_news.summary:
Google GTIG and iVerify identified the Coruna iOS exploit kit—a sophisticated, spyware-grade toolkit containing 23 exploits across five full exploit chains targeting iOS 13.0 through 17.2.1. Originally used by a commercial surveillance vendor’s client, it was later repurposed by Russian APTs and now appears in financially motivated campaigns, including crypto theft. The kit demonstrates an active secondary market for high-end zero-day exploits.

The proliferation of Coruna across threat actors—state and criminal—highlights how advanced capabilities are being commoditized and reused, significantly raising the bar for mobile threat defense. With no patch available for affected iOS versions, millions of devices remain vulnerable to zero-click compromise.

breaking_news.title: Iran-Linked MuddyWater Backdoored US Critical Infrastructure
breaking_news.summary:
Iranian state-affiliated group MuddyWater, linked to the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), embedded custom implants in the networks of a US bank, software firm, and airport beginning in early February 2026—prompted by US/Israeli military operations. The attackers used custom backdoors and maintained persistence across multiple sectors, indicating a deliberate, high-priority campaign to establish long-term access.

This intrusion demonstrates the escalation of cyber operations into kinetic conflict, with attackers targeting critical national infrastructure (CNI) for intelligence gathering and potential disruption. The compromise of financial, software, and transportation systems raises serious concerns about supply chain persistence and the viability of traditional network segmentation.

breaking_news.title: Tycoon 2FA Phishing-as-a-Service Platform Dismantled
breaking_news.summary:
Europol, Microsoft, and global partners dismantled Tycoon 2FA, a major phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform that enabled adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks to bypass MFA across hundreds of thousands of organizations monthly. Microsoft seized 330 domains powering the platform, while law enforcement in six countries executed raids. Tycoon 2FA accounted for ~62% of all phishing attacks by mid-2025 and was used to target healthcare, education, and government sectors.

The takedown disrupts one of the most effective tools for credential theft in the post-MFA era. Its removal significantly raises the barrier for less-sophisticated threat actors, though copycat platforms are likely to emerge. The operation underscores the importance of cross-industry collaboration in dismantling PhaaS infrastructure.

breaking_news.title: Bing AI Promoted Fake OpenClaw Repo Delivering Info-Stealers
breaking_news.summary:
Microsoft Bing’s AI-enhanced search feature promoted fake GitHub repositories for the OpenClaw AI agent, directing users to malicious installers that deployed GhostSocks proxy malware and information stealers. The campaign exploited trust in AI search results and GitHub’s reputation to deliver payloads via deceptive commands, affecting both enterprise and individual users.

This incident reveals how AI search interfaces can be weaponized to amplify phishing and malware distribution at scale. It highlights a new attack surface where legitimate AI tools become vectors for credential theft and network compromise, demanding new detection strategies beyond traditional URL filtering.

breaking_news.title: Phobos Ransomware Admin Pleads Guilty, Faces 20 Years
breaking_news.summary:
Russian national Evgenii Ptitsyn pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy for his role in the Phobos ransomware operation, which attacked over 1,000 public and private entities worldwide and extorted more than $16 million in ransom payments since November 2020. His extradition from South Korea and guilty plea mark a significant law enforcement victory against ransomware infrastructure operators.

While Phobos affiliates remain active, the dismantling of its core administration disrupts the RaaS ecosystem and may impact affiliate operations. The case underscores the increasing willingness of jurisdictions to cooperate in prosecuting ransomware operators and the long-term legal consequences for key players.

breaking_news.title: Ukraine Conflict Drives Surge in Hacktivist DDoS Attacks
breaking_news.summary:
Following the US-Israel military campaign against Iran (Operation Epic Fury), over 149 hacktivist DDoS attacks hit 110 organizations across 16 countries in just three days. Radware reported that two groups—Keymous+ and DieNet—drove nearly 70% of the activity, targeting government, financial, and infrastructure entities in the Middle East and Europe.

This surge illustrates how geopolitical conflicts rapidly translate into cyber retaliation, overwhelming defenses with volumetric attacks and increasing the risk of collateral damage to neutral entities. Organizations must prepare for sustained, politically motivated DDoS campaigns during periods of international tension.
affected_entities: Multiple government and financial institutions in Middle East and Europe

breaking_news.title: Amazon AWS Data Centers Damaged by Iranian Drone Strikes
breaking_news.summary:
Iranian drone strikes damaged three AWS data centers in the UAE and one in Bahrain, causing widespread cloud outages across the Middle East. Amazon confirmed the physical infrastructure was directly impacted, disrupting services for countless enterprises relying on AWS for critical operations—including healthcare, finance, and government agencies.

This marks a rare instance of kinetic warfare directly damaging cloud infrastructure, blurring the lines between physical and cyber conflict. The incident exposes the vulnerability of hyperscaler facilities to geopolitical violence and raises urgent questions about geographic redundancy and disaster recovery planning for cloud-native organizations.

unique_affected_entities: Multiple government and financial institutions in Middle East and Europe
